Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Each action moves the white character 3 cells in that direction. - White char now at rows 14-28, cols 14-28. Red feet at rows 29-31. - Green frame at rows 29-49, cols 41-49 with interior at ~rows 32-46, cols 44-46. - Need to move character toward green frame: ~9 RIGHT and ~5 DOWN from current position.
- Goal model: Move the white character to interact with the green frame (possibly enter it or match its position).
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT likely move the white character. Need to probe.
- Plan: Batch RIGHT moves to approach the green frame horizontally, then DOWN to align vertically.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 44 actions; a strong score needs about 29 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: I see the image looks very similar to an earlier state. Let me check the green count and try to find a pattern.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green total: {sum(n['pixels'] for n in greens)} in {len(greens)} pieces")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] != 'S':
        minr = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        maxr = max(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        minc = min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        maxc = max(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        print(f"id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows={minr}-{maxr} cols={minc}-{maxc}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Green went from 108 to 54! So SPACE reduced green from 108 to 54. This is the same state I was in before (green=54, character at rows 32-46, red on left and bottom).
Let me track the full history:
- Green 108, red on right -> SPACE -> Green 54, red on left/bottom
So SPACE toggles between two states: 108 and 54 green.
But earlier I saw green at 45, 72, 90, 99, 135, 144... So the state isn't just about SPACE toggling.
Wait, let me look at the character position. The character is at rows 32-46, cols 38-52. The green pieces are:
- rows 29-31 (27px) - above character
- rows 35-37, cols 41-43 (9px) - inside character (left)
- rows 35-37, cols 47-49 (9px) - inside character (right)
- rows 47-49, cols 44-46 (9px) - below character
Total: 54px
Red blocks:
- rows 41-43, cols 38-40 (9px) - left side
- rows 47-49, cols 41-43 (9px) - bottom left
- rows 47-49, cols 47-49 (9px) - bottom right
So the character has red on the left and bottom. This is the "inverted" orientation.
Now, when I press SPACE, the character rotates and green changes. The pattern seems to be:
- When character is in one orientation, green = 54
- When character is in the other orientation, green = 108
But earlier I saw green at 45, which is less than 54. So there must be other factors.
Let me think about what the goal could be. The minimum green I've seen is 45. Maybe the goal is to get green to 0? Or maybe the goal is to get the character to a specific position/orientation?
Actually, let me reconsider the whole approach. I've been treating this as a "reduce green" puzzle, but maybe the goal is different. Let me look at the overall game structure.
The game has a white character that moves and rotates. There are green pieces scattered around. Maybe the goal is to "collect" all green pieces by having the character overlap them, then pressing SPACE to "absorb" them.
But the green count oscillates, which suggests SPACE toggles between two states rather than permanently removing green.
Wait, let me look at the history more carefully. When green went from 90 to 45 (earlier), what was the character position?
Actually, I don't have the full history of positions. Let me try a different approach: instead of randomly moving, let me try to systematically explore all possible character positions and orientations to find the one that minimizes green or completes the level.
But that would take too many actions. Let me think about what makes sense.
Looking at the current state:
- Character at rows 32-46, cols 38-52
- Green: 54 pixels
- Red on left and bottom
The green pieces are arranged in a U-shape: top bar (29-31), two middle pieces (35-37), and bottom piece (47-49). The character overlaps with the middle pieces but not the top or bottom.
Maybe I need to move the character so it overlaps with ALL green pieces, then press SPACE to absorb them all at once.
The character is 15 rows tall (32-46) and 15 columns wide (38-52). The green pieces span rows 29-49 and cols 41-49.
To overlap with all green, the character needs to cover rows 29-49. But the character is only 15 rows tall, so it can't cover all 21 rows at once.
Unless... the character's effective "collection area" is larger than its visible body. Or maybe I need to collect green pieces in multiple steps.
Let me try a different approach: move the character UP to cover the top green (29-31), then SPACE. If the pattern holds, this should reduce green.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Green: 54 (same as before)
# Character at rows 32-46, red on left/bottom
# Green pieces: top (29-31), middle (35-37), bottom (47-49)
# Let me try UP to cover top green, then SPACE
action(['UP','SPACE'], intent='solve')
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
